## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

  Reflective audit pace at the tail of BB. Walk the changes landed across BB's paces and determine which RBS0 (and adjacent veiled-spec) sections fell out of sync with code/behavior, then update them.

  RBS0 (and any directly-implicated sibling spec files under `rbk/vov_veiled/`) reflect the behavior shipped during BB. Each touched section either updated or explicitly judged still-correct. No silent drift left behind for the next heat.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - Read-only against code; spec edits only.

  ## Out of scope
  
  - Restructuring RBS0 beyond what BB's changes require.

  ## Known sync points (running list — append as paces land)
  
  - **`RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc`** (from BBAAc inline fix on the JWT-SA probe — IAM-policy-read propagation race absorption). The procedural spec describes the OLD semantic: "all iterations must pass; HTTP 403 → fatal access denied; HTTP 401 → fatal token rejected." New code semantic in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`: bounded propagation-absorbing retry — first 2xx wins, HTTP 403 retries within `max_attempts × delay_ms` budget, all other codes (including 401) fall to a single fail-fast catch-all with body-summary diagnostic via `zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture`. Sections needing rewrite: §"Execute Probe Iterations" (rename concept to *attempts*; rewrite "Evaluate Response" sub-section to reflect 2xx-wins / 403-retry / else-fatal); §"Display Summary" ("succeeded after «I» attempt(s)" instead of "«N»/«N» iterations passed"); the top NOTE block (intent shift from sustained-access verification to propagation-window absorption + verification); §"Validate Input Parameters" (`ITERATION_COUNT` description shifts to "max attempts — first 2xx wins"; `DELAY_MS` to "between retries"). Companion call-site change: `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` defaults shifted to 60 × 3000ms (≈180s budget) for SA roles only; Payor probe defaults and semantic unchanged.

content.text: Let me read the paddock from the gazette before presenting.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BB
2	
3	## Status — scope narrowed (260601)
4	
5	The release-qualification **machinery is built**: `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine, the
6	gauntlet suite, and the marshal-zero signature gate all shipped. What remained —
7	the actual pristine **runs**, the RELEASE.md **revision**, and the post-success
8	**cleanup-ceremony documentation** — was gated on "are we actually releasing,"
9	which is deferred. Those ideas now live in **₣BU rbk-mvp-final-release-prep**
10	(stabled, paddock-only) and will be slated fresh when release time comes.
11	
12	₣BB stays racing for **three tail items only** — ordinary engineering that gates
13	a clean pristine run but is not release ceremony:
14	
15	- Foundry account-state-invalid retry-tolerance bug fix (401/`ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` flap)
16	- Fast-tier shellcheck fold + parallelization + marshal-zero gate
17	- RBS0 spec-sync sweep (known sync point: RBSAJ probe semantic)
18	
19	Everything below is the design record of the machinery as built — retained as
20	the authoritative account of what shipped, not as open scope.
21	
22	## Context
23	
24	Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.
25	
26	This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.
27	
28	## Single operator prerequisite chain
29	
30	Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.
31	
32	## Entry contract — load-bearing
33	
34	`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:
35	
36	- Working tree clean
37	- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
38	- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
39	- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
40	- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
41	- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files
42	
43	Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.
44	
45	## Failure mode contract
46	
47	Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.
48	
49	## Release-branch execution contract
50	
51	Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.
52	
53	**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.
54	
55	**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.
56	
57	**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.
58	
59	Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.
60	
61	## Tier layering
62	
63	Three tiers, escalating cost:
64	
65	| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
66	|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
67	| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
68	| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
69	| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |
70	
71	`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.
72	
73	## Gauntlet test suite — design
74	
75	`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.
76	
77	**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.
78	
79	**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).
80	
81	**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:
82	
83	1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
84	2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
85	3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
86	4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
87	5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
88	6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
89	7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)
90	
91	No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.
92	
93	## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage
94	
95	The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.
96	
97	Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.
98	
99	**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.
100	
101	Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.
102	
103	Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.
104	
105	## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing
106	
107	Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:
108	
109	- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
110	- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.
111	
112	**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.
113	
114	Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.
115	
116	**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.
117	
118	## Suite-level fail-fast
119	
120	Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.
121	
122	## No-teardown decision
123	
124	The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:
125	
126	- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
127	- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
128	- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
129	- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.
130	
131	Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony moved to ₣BU (RELEASE.md revision), where the release-run and doc work now lives.
132	
133	## Operator scope
134	
135	Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.
136	
137	## Coupling to ₣A_
138	
139	Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.
140	
141	## Locked design decisions
142	
143	- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
144	- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
145	- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.
146	
147	## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh
148	
149	Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.
150	
151	**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.
152	
153	**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):
154	
155	- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
156	- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
157	- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
158	- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.
159	
160	Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.
161

# jjezs_paddock ₣BB

## Status — scope narrowed (260601)

The release-qualification **machinery is built**: `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine, the
gauntlet suite, and the marshal-zero signature gate all shipped. What remained —
the actual pristine **runs**, the RELEASE.md **revision**, and the post-success
**cleanup-ceremony documentation** — was gated on "are we actually releasing,"
which is deferred. Those ideas now live in **₣BU rbk-mvp-final-release-prep**
(stabled, paddock-only) and will be slated fresh when release time comes.

₣BB stays racing for **three tail items only** — ordinary engineering that gates
a clean pristine run but is not release ceremony:

- Foundry account-state-invalid retry-tolerance bug fix (401/`ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID` flap)
- Fast-tier shellcheck fold + parallelization + marshal-zero gate
- RBS0 spec-sync sweep (known sync point: RBSAJ probe semantic)

Everything below is the design record of the machinery as built — retained as
the authoritative account of what shipped, not as open scope.

## Context

Release qualification gap surfaced by the ₣A_ depot-regen heat. Every existing test tier (`rbw-tf` fast, `rbw-tr` release) tolerates accumulated depot state, so silent first-build assumptions slip through. Recent spooks — kludge-aware-charge-prereq (k-prefixed hallmarks at GAR), ZRBOB_PROJECT (compose project name without runtime prefix), rbob_charged_predicate prefix omission, reliquary integrity-broken on negative test — all share a property: they only manifest on first-build paths, and none were caught until live verification surfaced them.

This heat constructs `rbw-tP` QualifyPristine — a third tier that **refuses to run** unless marshal-zero state was just committed. The refusal is enforced by the test itself, not by ceremony or operator discipline.

## Single operator prerequisite chain

Operator has exactly one prerequisite chain: confirm Payor health → marshal-zero → commit → run `rbw-tP`. Payor OAuth is the only credential the operator must have ready; everything else (governor, retriever, director SAs and their RBRA files) is **minted by the qualification itself**, not restored from backup.

## Entry contract — load-bearing

`rbw-tP` fails-fast unless ALL of the following hold:

- Working tree clean
- HEAD commit is a marshal-zero commit (detectable signature baked into `rblm_zero` per BBAAB)
- RBRR fields are blank (prefixes, depot project ID, etc.)
- No RBRA credential files present (governor, retriever, director — all deleted by marshal-zero, recreated by the qualification)
- No hallmark fields populated in nameplates
- No depot-scoped fields populated in vessel rbrv.env files

Operator cannot skip the prerequisite. This is the property that makes the tier catch the silent-first-build bug class by construction.

## Failure mode contract

Mid-qualification failure means start-over-from-zero, not patch-and-continue. Documented explicitly in runbook to prevent the very accumulated-state bug class this tier exists to catch.

## Release-branch execution contract

Three properties bind every fixture and phase in this tier.

**Run on a release branch.** The release machinery is engineered for branch execution, not main. The branch will accumulate many commits across a successful pristine run. After a failed run, the branch holds bygone commits that `rbw-MZ` does not touch — they remain as history; the next run starts from a fresh marshal-zero commit on top.

**Commits during the run are first-class.** Each step that mutates regime state (rbrr.env, rbra files, vessel rbrv.env, etc.) commits the change. Container-recipe work has a documented cognitive failure mode: humans lose track of where they are in long sequences. The growing commit trail is the operator's mental anchor against that.

**Stop on very first failure, cleanly and clearly.** A step fails → fixture stops → operator stops → debugging happens immediately on the failed branch. Stderr names the failed step; fixture exits non-zero. Recovery is `rbw-MZ` + retry on a fresh marshal-zero. No graceful degradation, no recovery branches.

Engineering scaffolding for any other shape — partial-run cleanup, soft-delete tolerance, multi-mode failure handling, recovery-time orphan inspection, diagnostic-redundancy — is non-load-bearing and does not belong in pristine-tier code. The single mechanical path is the entire surface.

## Tier layering

Three tiers, escalating cost:

| Colophon | Frontispiece | Cost | Entry contract |
|----------|--------------|------|----------------|
| `rbw-tf` | QualifyFast | seconds | none |
| `rbw-tr` | QualifyRelease | minutes | none (accumulated state OK) |
| `rbw-tP` | QualifyPristine *(new)* | ~1 hour + cloud $ | marshal-zero just committed |

`rbw-tP` is THE release gate. `rbw-tr` becomes the pre-pristine smoke test — cheap-and-frequent for development confidence; pristine for actual release.

## Gauntlet test suite — design

`rbw-tP` exec's the **gauntlet suite** — a TestSuite (not a custom orchestrator script) composed of fixtures sequenced from marshal-zero state through canonical-credentialed state to crucible verification. The TestSuite mechanism is existing infrastructure (`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.{fast,service,crucible,complete}.sh`); the gauntlet suite extends it.

**Suite-of-fixtures, not orchestrator-script.** The original construction plan (custom bash orchestrator with inline tabtarget calls) was abandoned in favor of suite composition. Each section of the release-qualification sequence becomes a fixture; the suite composes them in order.

**Naming hygiene.** "Gauntlet" is the suite name; "pristine" is reserved for the §1 pristine-lifecycle fixture and informally for tier vocabulary. The suite name does not appear at fixture or case level — distinct stratification across naming layers (suite ≠ fixture ≠ case).

**State ladder, fixtures along it.** The suite walks a state ladder no single fixture covers:

1. enrollment-validation (preflight, state-indifferent)
2. pristine-lifecycle (§1: marshal-zero gate + throwaway depot/SA lifecycle)
3. canonical-establish (§2: canonical depot levy + governor mantle + retriever invest + director invest + IAM propagation wait)
4. canonical-onboarding-sequence (§3: reliquary inscribe + per-vessel hallmark ordain — handbook-driven build walks per BBAAf)
5. regime-validation, regime-smoke (post-§3, regimes now populated)
6. four-mode (cloud-build mode coverage)
7. tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml (§4: per-crucible test fixtures, vessel-exists preconditions per BBAAs)

No automatic teardown — see "No-teardown decision" below.

## Dual purpose: handbook-regression coverage

The gauntlet's primary purpose is release qualification (catch silent first-build assumptions). Its **secondary purpose** is automated handbook-regression coverage: ensure operator-facing onboarding handbook tracks (`rbh*`) stay in sync with working code so future operators following the prescribed steps aren't surprised by drift.

Build-side handbook coverage lives in BBAAf (canonical-onboarding-sequence): each ordain case walks the corresponding director-mode handbook track (`rbw-Odf` conjure / `rbw-Oda` airgap / `rbw-Odb` bind / `rbw-Odg` graft) rather than calling inscribe/ordain machinery directly.

**Build/run split — load-bearing.** Handbook walks cover the BUILD step (vessel construction) and stop when the hallmark lands in GAR. Run-side fixtures (per-crucible: tadmor/moriah/srjcl/pluml) stay as direct test fixtures with vessel-exists preconditions, NOT handbook walks. The split keeps run-side reusable against any vessel source — coupling them to handbook prose breaks reusability.

Drift between fixtures and handbooks is accepted future maintenance: whoever changes one is responsible for updating the other; the gauntlet's job is to surface drift, not prevent it by construction.

Run-side handbook coverage (`rbw-Ots`, the rack portion of `rbw-Ofc`) is explicitly out of scope for this heat.

## Fixture disposition — Independent vs StateProgressing

Each fixture declares a disposition flag in Rust, controlling engine behavior:

- **Independent** — cases are self-contained; suite-order is informational. Engine permits keep-going mode for surveying.
- **StateProgressing** — case N's success establishes preconditions for case N+1. Engine refuses keep-going mode (incoherent: failed case leaves broken precondition). Fail-fast required.

**Default is Independent.** Existing fixtures (`enrollment-validation`, `regime-validation`, `regime-smoke`, `four-mode`, `tadmor`, `moriah`, `srjcl`, `pluml`) are Independent and require no tagging change. Only fixtures whose case-N success establishes case-N+1 preconditions get StateProgressing — currently `pristine-lifecycle`, `canonical-establish`, and `canonical-onboarding-sequence`.

Suite-level disposition derives from membership: any StateProgressing fixture in the suite makes the suite state-progressing → suite-level keep-going refused.

**Per-case precondition probes.** Each case in a StateProgressing fixture probes its precondition at start. If state matches expectation, run. If not, refuse with operator-actionable diagnostic naming the expected state and the closest fixture that produces it. This enables safe a-la-carte single-case rerun — the engine doesn't need to know the case's history; the case's probe enforces.

## Suite-level fail-fast

Across all suites — not just gauntlet — any fixture failure stops subsequent fixtures from running automatically. State-progressing suites require this for correctness; independent suites adopt it for the simpler mental model. Operators who want full surveying run individual fixtures separately.

## No-teardown decision

The gauntlet suite omits an automatic teardown phase. After green tally, the canonical depot, SAs, hallmarks, and RBRA files persist for operator inspection. Deliberate trade-off:

- **Inspect-after-success** is genuine operator value — browse canonical depot in GCP console, inspect GAR contents, run ad-hoc verification a test sequence cannot anticipate.
- **Cleanup is operator-driven** — manual sequence is `rbw-fA` per vessel + `rbw-arD` + `rbw-adD` + `rbw-dU` (governor cleanup folds into unmake per BBAAN). All existing tabtargets; no new infrastructure.
- **Failed runs don't accumulate cleanup debt** — under fail-fast, teardown wouldn't run anyway; the no-teardown decision only changes behavior on full success.
- **Cost visibility** — pending-delete soft-deleted projects accumulate per run (RELEASE.md accepts this); not changed by teardown decision.

Documentation of the post-success cleanup ceremony moved to ₣BU (RELEASE.md revision), where the release-run and doc work now lives.

## Operator scope

Single operator (project lead). Not designed for multi-operator workflow. The README.md release section remains the runbook for the gauntlet itself; the dual-purpose handbook-regression coverage protects the broader operator surface (onboarding tracks under `rbh*`) from drift.

## Coupling to ₣A_

Cutover work from ₣A_ informing `rbw-tP`'s sequence has landed (BBAAM depot-identity-collapse). ₣A_'s remaining paces run independently of BB.

## Locked design decisions

- **Depot identity collapse**: RBRR collapses to `RBRR_DEPOT_MONIKER`; `RBRR_CLOUD_PREFIX` flows through depot-affiliated resources; per-moniker depot fact-files; pristine-fixture moniker autodetect lives in Rust (`rbtdrp_pristine.rs`), not payor.
- **Cult-verb naming**: SA domain muster→**roster**; image domain muster→**audit**. Domain-exclusive split; lowercase tail letters preserved for no-cloud-change observation colophons.
- **Build/run split**: handbook walks cover build only; per-crucible fixtures stay reusable as direct tests.

## Calibrant fixture family — BBAAh

Synthetic Rust fixtures with deterministic verdicts, registered in the manifest by BBAAh. The family exists to give ₣BC `rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test` deterministic targets for bash-blackbox driving and manifest-coverage validation; here in ₣BB only the foundation lands.

**Anchor word: `calibrant`.** Module `rbtdrl_calibrant.rs` (classifier `l` for caLibrant since `c` is owned by crucible). Replaces the dormant `rbtdrd_dummy.rs` placeholder. Test counterpart `rbtdtl_calibrant.rs`. Internal-only vocabulary; not end-user-facing.

**Fixture catalog** (Rust, manifest-registered):

- `calibrant-verdicts` (Independent) — pass / fail / skip / pass_with_output. Verdict-path coverage. The case-written file (`output.txt`) is distinct from the engine-auto `trace.txt`.
- `calibrant-fail-fast` (Independent, multi-section) — fixture-internal fail-fast across cases and across sections; "not_reached" cases write sentinel files used as absence-of-side-effect assertions.
- `calibrant-progressing` (StateProgressing) — exercises `rbtdrb_Probe` diagnostic format and `rbtdre_resolve_fail_fast` policy gate.
- `calibrant-sentinel` (Independent) — single case writing a known file when it runs. Used for suite-level fail-fast verification.

Suite: `calibrant` = [verdicts, fail-fast, sentinel]. Sentinel-after-fail verifies cross-fixture fail-fast contract.

Downstream consumption — bash testbench, manifest-coverage runtime checks, coverage-validation fixtures and driver — lives in ₣BC.

## References

- ₣BU rbk-mvp-final-release-prep — the release runs, RELEASE.md revision, and cleanup-ceremony docs now live here (stabled until release time)
- ₣A_ rbk-mvp-3-resource-prefix-and-depot-regen — surfaced the gap
- ₣BC rbk-mvp-3-theurge-self-test — calibrant downstream consumers
- `tt/rbw-tr.QualifyRelease.sh` — current release qualify; layered alongside, not replaced
- `Tools/rbk/rblm_cli.sh` — `rbw-MZ` zeroes local regime; marshal-zero signature baked here per BBAAB
- `.claude/commands/rbk-prep-release.md` — upstream contribution ceremony; pristine-pass becomes a precondition

## Wrap criterion

## Constraints

- Read-only against code; spec edits only.
- Scope is BB's changes — not a full RBS0 audit.

## Out of scope

- Restructuring RBS0 beyond what BB's changes require.
- Spec touches outside the veiled-spec family unless directly implicated.

## Known sync points (running list — append as paces land)

- **`RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc`** (from BBAAc inline fix on the JWT-SA probe — IAM-policy-read propagation race absorption). The procedural spec describes the OLD semantic: "all iterations must pass; HTTP 403 → fatal access denied; HTTP 401 → fatal token rejected." New code semantic in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh`: bounded propagation-absorbing retry — first 2xx wins, HTTP 403 retries within `max_attempts × delay_ms` budget, all other codes (including 401) fall to a single fail-fast catch-all with body-summary diagnostic via `zrbgv_jq_error_message_capture`. Sections needing rewrite: §"Execute Probe Iterations" (rename concept to *attempts*; rewrite "Evaluate Response" sub-section to reflect 2xx-wins / 403-retry / else-fatal); §"Display Summary" ("succeeded after «I» attempt(s)" instead of "«N»/«N» iterations passed"); the top NOTE block (intent shift from sustained-access verification to propagation-window absorption + verification); §"Validate Input Parameters" (`ITERATION_COUNT` description shifts to "max attempts — first 2xx wins"; `DELAY_MS` to "between retries"). Companion call-site change: `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` defaults shifted to 60 × 3000ms (≈180s budget) for SA roles only; Payor probe defaults and semantic unchanged.

**Pace goal (₢BBAAq — rbs0-sync-sweep-from-bb-changes):** Walk everything BB landed and reconcile RBS0 — plus any directly-implicated sibling specs under `rbk/vov_veiled/` — back to the behavior actually shipped, so no silent doc-vs-code drift carries into the next heat.

**Heat context — ₣BB rbk-mvp-release-qualification** (racing, 59/62):
The release-qualification *machinery* shipped (`rbw-tP` QualifyPristine, the gauntlet suite, the marshal-zero signature gate). Scope was narrowed on 260601 to three tail items only; the actual release runs + RELEASE.md revision moved to ₣BU. This is the last of those three tail items — a reflective spec-sync sweep, not new machinery.

**Full docket:**
- **Character:** Reflective audit. Deliberate read-and-compare. Source of truth is code/handbook surface *as it stands now*; RBS0 is reconciled to it.
- **Wrap criterion:** RBS0 + directly-implicated `rbk/vov_veiled/` siblings reflect BB's shipped behavior. Each touched section either updated or explicitly judged still-correct. No silent drift left.
- **Constraints:** Read-only against code; spec edits only. Scope is *BB's changes*, not a full RBS0 audit.
- **Out of scope:** Restructuring RBS0 beyond what BB requires; spec touches outside the veiled-spec family unless directly implicated.
- **Known sync point (seeded):** `RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe.adoc` — from the BBAAc JWT-SA probe fix. Spec still describes the OLD semantic ("all iterations must pass; 403→fatal; 401→fatal"); code in `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` now does bounded propagation-absorbing retry (first 2xx wins, 403 retries within budget, everything else single fail-fast). Four sections flagged for rewrite, plus the `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` 60×3000ms default shift (SA roles only).

**Name assessment:** Silks `rbs0-sync-sweep-from-bb-changes` fits the docket cleanly — no rename needed.

**Note on state:** ₢BBAAq is still `rough`, not armed. The orient's seeded sync-point list is one concrete entry; the real surface is the file-touch bitmap — RBS0 alone was touched by ~20 of BB's 61 paces, and several sibling specs (RBSDY, RBSAE, RBSAC, the director/retriever invest/divest/roster cluster, RBSDL depot-list, etc.) ride along.

**Proposed approach:**
- Derive the candidate spec set from the bitmap — every `*.adoc` under `rbk/vov_veiled/` that a BB pace touched, anchored by RBS0 itself.
- For each candidate, diff the spec's described behavior against the current code surface; classify as *needs-edit* / *still-correct*, recording the verdict either way (wrap criterion demands explicit judgment, not silent pass).
- Apply edits, starting from the seeded RBSAJ rewrite, then the broader set.

**Execution strategy:** Opus-tier — this is read-and-compare judgment, not mechanical. The candidate-discovery and per-spec drift assessment fan out cleanly across files (independent reads), so I'd lean on parallel Explore agents to survey drift, then do the edits myself with the conclusions in hand. Given the breadth (~20+ candidate specs), this is a real chunk of work, not a quick pass.

Want me to arm ₢BBAAq and start with the discovery sweep (build the candidate set + drift verdicts), or scope it down first — e.g. RBSAJ + RBS0 only, deferring the sibling cluster?

aiTitle: Evaluate project scope and audit effort sizing

content.text:
I think your instinct is right, and I'd push it one step further: **size first, and treat the sizing as the only thing we commit to right now.** Here's my reasoning.

The docket as written conflates two things that have very different costs:
1. **Surveying** the drift — read-only, parallelizable, cheap, bounded.
2. **Repairing** the drift — edits, judgment per section, unknown magnitude.

We genuinely don't know which world we're in yet. The bitmap is suggestive but not conclusive — a pace touching a spec often means that pace *already synced* it (code + spec landed together). The dangerous drift is the inverse: specs whose underlying behavior shifted but which *weren't* touched by the responsible pace, plus partial syncs. So "20+ touched specs" is an upper bound on candidates, not a measure of actual drift. Could be that most are clean and this is a half-day pace; could be a dozen genuine rewrites and it's a heat.

**Recommendation:** redocket ₢BBAAq down to a pure **sizing audit** — read-only, produces a *drift ledger*: one row per candidate spec with a verdict (`in-sync` / `drifted-minor` / `drifted-major`) and a one-line note on what shifted. No edits. The seeded RBSAJ entry becomes row one. That ledger is cheap to produce and is exactly the artifact that answers your question.

Then the ledger gates the container decision — and I'd *not* pre-decide it:
- **Small** (RBSAJ + a handful of minor touch-ups) → just finish the repairs inside ₢BBAAq, wrap, done within BB.
- **Large** (many genuine rewrites) → it's a heat. The ledger seeds the new heat's paddock; ₢BBAAq wraps as "audit complete, repair relocated to ₣XX," or the audit transfers in as that heat's first pace.

The nice property: the audit is load-bearing *regardless* of which world we land in. It's not throwaway scoping — the drift ledger is the thing the repair work consumes either way. So we lose nothing by sizing first, and we avoid committing BB to swallowing an unknown.

Concretely, I'd reslate ₢BBAAq to the sizing audit, arm it, and fan out Explore agents — one per cluster of related specs (foundry, director/retriever SA lifecycle, depot, crucible/charge, access-probe) — each comparing spec prose against current code and reporting drift verdicts. I synthesize into the ledger, we read it together, and *then* decide the container.

Want me to redocket ₢BBAAq to the sizing-audit shape and run the survey?

content: Goal: reconcile RBS0 and sibling specs to what heat ₣BB actually shipped. We agreed to size the effort first via a read-only drift audit before deciding if it's a pace or a whole heat. Next: redocket ₢BBAAq to the sizing-audit shape and run the survey.

Code (`rbgv_jwt_sa_probe`) implements BB's propagation-absorbing bounded retry: first 2xx (200/206) wins and returns, 403 retries within the count budget then dies "propagation budget exhausted" with jq body summary, all other codes (incl. 401) fall to one fail-fast catch-all with body summary. The entire Execute-Iterations / Evaluate-Response / Display-Summary structure is wrong; `--count` is now max-attempts not iteration-count. | L |

content.text:
Survey's done — ledger recorded to ₢BBAAq's landing. Here's the sizing verdict, and it answers your question cleanly.

## Is this a whole heat? No.

The drift is **shallow and concentrated**, not broad. ~35 specs surveyed; the large majority are already in-sync — they were synced by the paces that changed them as BB landed. The foundry/ark cluster (conjure/graft/vouch/summon/enshrine/kludge/hygiene/tally) is pristine. The RBS0 spine itself is ~95% clean — cult-verb renames, depot-identity collapse, reliquary model, GAR tokens, rbrv anchor format, JWT propagation profile are all correctly reflected.

## What actually drifted

**Two real repairs:**
- **RBSAJ** (the seeded one) — `L`, genuine rewrite. Spec still describes the old all-iterations-must-pass probe; code is the propagation-absorbing retry.
- **SA invest/divest cluster** (RBSDK, RBSRK, RBSDD, RBSRD) — four `L` rows, but **one conceptual drift**: specs describe singleton-per-role, code is identity-parameterized. The generated command reference (`rbw-arI/adI/…`, folio = param1) corroborates that parameterized is the *intended* shipped design — so this is spec→code reconciliation, not a code bug. Four files, one fix applied four times.

**A scatter of ~12 small touch-ups** — mostly stale constant-name references (RBSIA/RBSIR kindle prose, RBSDL `RBGP_*`→`RBCC_*`, RBSDI substitution name, RBSTB trigger-prefix), one-line cross-refs, and missing-field additions (RBSRV `RBRV_RELIQUARY`). Three of these (RBSRR, RBSAA, RBSAP) the agents flagged as **largely BB-independent pre-existing drift** — arguably outside this pace's scope ("BB's changes, not a full RBS0 audit").

## One correction the audit surfaced

The seeded docket's call-site claim was **wrong**: it named `rbte_engine.sh::rbte_probe` (60×3000ms). That file is the theurge test engine — no probe in it. The probe is `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh::rbgv_jwt_sa_probe`. Worth noting because it would have sent the RBSAJ repair to the wrong file.

## Recommendation for the container

This fits inside BB as **1–3 repair paces** — no new heat. I'd shape it as:
- **Pace 1 (`M`):** RBSAJ probe rewrite — the one substantive piece.
- **Pace 2 (`M`):** SA invest/divest model correction across the four sibling specs — coherent, mechanical once the model is stated.
- **Pace 3 (`S`, optional):** the in-scope cosmetic cluster (constant-names, cross-refs, RBSRV field, RBS0 depot-list-update §). Defer or drop the three BB-independent ones unless you want them swept now.

Want me to reslate ₢BBAAq into the repair work directly (it's read-only now — I'd either repurpose it to Pace 1 and chivvy the others after it, or wrap it as the audit and slate fresh repair paces)? Your call on scope — particularly whether the three BB-independent drifts (RBSRR, RBSAA, RBSAP) are in or out.

# jjezs_slate access-probe-spec-sync

## Character

Spec→code reconciliation for the two access-probe procedural specs. RBSAJ is a
structural rewrite; RBSAO is a small handler delta. Mechanical once the shipped
semantic is stated.

## Source of truth

`rbk/rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` — `rbgv_jwt_sa_probe` (JWT SA) and the Payor OAuth
probe (`rbgv_payor_oauth_probe` / `zrbgv_payor_crm_probe_once`).

## RBSAJ-access_jwt_probe — rewrite

Replace the old all-iterations-must-pass model (403→fatal, 401→fatal, "N/N
iterations passed") with the shipped propagation-absorbing bounded retry: first
2xx (200/206) wins and returns; HTTP 403 retries within the attempt-count budget
then dies "propagation budget exhausted" with a jq body summary; all other codes
(including 401) fall to one fail-fast catch-all with body summary. `--count` is
max-attempts (not iteration-count); `--delay-ms` is between retries. Rewrite the
Execute-Iterations / Evaluate-Response / Display-Summary sections and the top
NOTE (intent shifts from sustained-access verification to propagation-window
absorption). The 60×3000ms SA-role defaults are set at the tabtarget/dispatch
call-site, NOT in rbgv (rbgv defaults count=1/delay_ms=0) — describe accordingly.
Payor OAuth probe semantic is unchanged.

## RBSAO-access_oauth_probe — small fix

Spec carries a dedicated HTTP 404 ("Payor project not found") handler the code
lacks; code folds 404 into the generic catch-all. Reconcile spec to that.

## Wrap criterion

Both specs describe the shipped probe behavior. No reference to the removed
all-pass/iteration model or the phantom 404 handler remains.
